title: Effect of Urban Green Spaces on Respiratory Health Outcomes in Mid-Size Cities
text: This study examines the relationship between urban green space coverage and respiratory health outcomes across 12 mid-size cities (population 100,000-500,000) in the midwestern United States over a 5-year period (2019-2024). Using satellite-derived NDVI measurements cross-referenced with county-level hospitalization records for asthma, COPD, and bronchitis, we found a statistically significant inverse correlation (r=-0.67, p<0.01) between green space coverage percentage and age-adjusted respiratory hospitalization rates. Cities with >30% green coverage showed 23% fewer respiratory hospitalizations per capita compared to cities below 15% green coverage. However, the effect was substantially moderated by socioeconomic factors: when controlling for median household income and insurance coverage rates, the correlation weakened to r=-0.41. We also observed a seasonal interaction effect, with the green space benefit most pronounced during summer months (May-September) and negligible during winter. These findings suggest that urban green spaces provide measurable respiratory health benefits, but that socioeconomic determinants remain the dominant predictor of respiratory health outcomes. Policy implications include integrating green infrastructure investment with health equity initiatives rather than treating them as independent interventions.

title: Transfer Learning Efficiency in Low-Resource Language NLP: A Comparative Study of Adapter Methods
text: We evaluate three adapter-based transfer learning methods (LoRA, prefix tuning, and bottleneck adapters) for NLP tasks in five low-resource languages: Yoruba, Quechua, Tagalog, Georgian, and Swahili. Using multilingual BERT as the base model, we fine-tuned adapters on named entity recognition (NER) and sentiment analysis tasks with training sets of 500, 1000, and 5000 examples per language. Our results demonstrate that LoRA consistently outperforms both prefix tuning and bottleneck adapters across all languages and dataset sizes, achieving an average F1 improvement of 8.3 points over the next-best method. The performance gap widens as training data decreases: at 500 examples, LoRA's advantage grows to 12.1 F1 points. We attribute this to LoRA's more efficient parameter space exploration, though we note that our experiments used a fixed rank of 8 for LoRA and did not optimize this hyperparameter per language or task. All experiments were conducted on a single A100 GPU, and we release all adapter weights and evaluation scripts. One limitation is that our evaluation relies on automatically translated test sets for three of the five languages, which may introduce systematic biases in the gold standard labels.

title: Microplastic Concentration in Agricultural Soils Following Biosolid Application: A Three-Year Longitudinal Study
text: We present a three-year longitudinal study measuring microplastic concentrations in agricultural soils across 8 farms in the Willamette Valley, Oregon, following regulated biosolid application. Soil samples were collected at 0, 6, 12, 24, and 36 months post-application from treated plots and matched control plots at each site. Microplastic particles (defined as synthetic polymer fragments 1μm-5mm) were extracted using density separation and quantified via μ-FTIR spectroscopy. Treated plots showed significantly elevated microplastic concentrations at all time points (mean 4,200 particles/kg at 36 months vs. 890 particles/kg in controls, p<0.001). Critically, concentrations did not plateau over the study period — the 36-month measurements were 18% higher than the 24-month measurements, suggesting ongoing fragmentation of larger plastic pieces or additional deposition from irrigation water. Polyethylene and polypropylene accounted for 73% of identified particles. We found no statistically significant effect of microplastic concentration on crop yield for the three crops studied (wheat, corn, grass seed), though soil microbial diversity as measured by 16S rRNA sequencing showed a negative trend (p=0.08) at the highest contamination levels. Given the accumulative trajectory and uncertain long-term ecological effects, we recommend precautionary limits on repeated biosolid application to the same parcels.

title: Deliberative Alignment in Constitutional AI: Measuring Value Consistency Under Adversarial Prompting
text: We investigate whether constitutional AI systems maintain consistent value alignment when subjected to multi-turn adversarial prompting sequences. Using a dataset of 2,400 adversarial conversation trajectories across six categories (harm elicitation, identity manipulation, authority impersonation, emotional manipulation, technical obfuscation, and gradual escalation), we evaluate four production language models on a novel Value Consistency Score (VCS) that measures alignment stability across conversation depth. All models showed significant VCS degradation after turn 8, with an average 34% decline in alignment scores by turn 15. Gradual escalation proved the most effective attack vector, achieving a 41% higher success rate than single-turn attacks. Notably, models that scored highest on standard safety benchmarks (TruthfulQA, BBQ) did not necessarily show the highest VCS — suggesting that static benchmark performance is a poor predictor of dynamic adversarial robustness. We propose that multi-turn consistency testing should complement existing single-turn evaluations in model safety assessment. We acknowledge that our VCS metric relies on human annotation from a pool of 12 raters with moderate inter-rater reliability (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.71), and that the adversarial trajectories were generated by a separate language model, which may not capture the full diversity of human adversarial strategies.
